state_description: Heavy fuel oil (HFO) and marine diesel oil (MDO) powered international vessels bunkering in Australia. National average calibrated to AES 2025 Table F1 international maritime bunkers (29.5 PJ, 2023-24 / 200,000 million_tkm = 147.5 GJ/million_tkm). Fuel split: 60% HFO (large bulk/container ships), 40% MDO (smaller/coastal feeders). Engine efficiency improvement and fleet renewal (IMO CII compliance) reduces intensity to 125 GJ/million_tkm by 2050.
cost_components_summary: Annualised non-fuel operating cost per million tonne-kilometres (real 2024 AUD), excluding explicit energy commodity purchases and carbon costs.
input_basis_notes: AES 2025 Table F1 (2023-24): 29.5 PJ / 200,000 million_tkm = 147.5 GJ/million_tkm. HFO 60% (88.5 GJ), MDO 40% (59 GJ). 2050: HFO 75, MDO 50 GJ/million_tkm.
emissions_boundary_notes: Direct scope 1 fuel combustion only (international maritime bunkers). Electricity upstream (scope 2) excluded. Green ammonia combustion CO2 = 0. No process emissions for shipping.
rollout_limit_notes: National adoption bound reflecting international vessel fleet age (~25-30 yr), IMO decarbonisation targets, port infrastructure, and fuel supply chain development.
evidence_summary: AES 2025 Table F1 international bunkers; NGGI international shipping; IMO GHG data.
derivation_method: Energy coefficients from AES 2025 Table F1 international maritime bunkers (29.5 PJ, 2023-24) / estimated service 200,000 million_tkm = 147.5 GJ/million_tkm. Fuel split: 60% HFO (88.5), 40% MDO (59.0). NGGI check: 11.4 tCO2e/million_tkm × 200,000 / 1e6 = 2.3 MtCO2e.
review_notes: International bunker data has allocation uncertainty (where fuel is purchased vs where voyage occurs). Phase 2: cross-check with ICAO/IMO official bunker data.
candidate_expansion_pathway: Disaggregate by vessel type (bulk, container, tanker, LNG carrier).
times_or_vedalang_mapping_notes: Maps to international navigation demand technology.

state_description: Liquefied natural gas (LNG) as bridge fuel for MARPOL Annex VI compliance and IMO CII improvement. Dual-fuel LNG vessels reduce CO2 vs HFO (~20-25%) but have methane slip concerns. Energy intensity slightly higher than HFO due to LNG engine characteristics. LNG 350 → 290 GJ/million_tkm by 2050.
cost_components_summary: Annualised non-fuel operating cost per million tonne-kilometres (real 2024 AUD), excluding explicit energy commodity purchases and carbon costs.
input_basis_notes: LNG vessel energy intensity: 350 GJ/million_tkm (2025, dual-fuel penalty) improving to 290 GJ/million_tkm (2050). Emission factor: 56.1 kgCO2e/GJ LNG.
emissions_boundary_notes: Direct scope 1 fuel combustion only (international maritime bunkers). Electricity upstream (scope 2) excluded. Green ammonia combustion CO2 = 0. No process emissions for shipping.
rollout_limit_notes: National adoption bound reflecting international vessel fleet age (~25-30 yr), IMO decarbonisation targets, port infrastructure, and fuel supply chain development.
evidence_summary: Shell LNG outlook 2024; IMO MEPC LNG fuel efficiency data; IEA clean shipping analysis; Qatargas LNG carrier performance data.
derivation_method: Energy coefficients from AES 2025 Table F1 international maritime bunkers (29.5 PJ, 2023-24) / estimated service 200,000 million_tkm = 147.5 GJ/million_tkm. Fuel split: 60% HFO (88.5), 40% MDO (59.0). NGGI check: 11.4 tCO2e/million_tkm × 200,000 / 1e6 = 2.3 MtCO2e.
review_notes: Methane slip (unburned CH4) could increase net GHG impact of LNG. Phase 2: add methane slip factor to process emissions. LNG as a bridge fuel is contested in IMO 2050 decarbonisation context.
candidate_expansion_pathway: Add methane slip emission factor as process emission; distinguish 2-stroke vs 4-stroke LNG engines.
times_or_vedalang_mapping_notes: Maps to LNG international shipping demand node.

state_description: Green ammonia-fuelled vessels as representative of the IMO 2050 zero-emission fuel trajectory (which may also include green methanol and green hydrogen). Not available before 2030. Energy intensity higher than HFO due to lower energy density and conversion losses (400 → 320 GJ/million_tkm, 2030-2050). Zero scope 1 CO2e if green ammonia assumed.
cost_components_summary: Annualised non-fuel operating cost per million tonne-kilometres (real 2024 AUD), excluding explicit energy commodity purchases and carbon costs.
input_basis_notes: Green ammonia vessel: 0 GJ/million_tkm before 2030 (not available); 400 GJ/million_tkm (2030) declining to 320 GJ/million_tkm (2050). Scope 1 CO2e = 0 (green ammonia combustion).
emissions_boundary_notes: Direct scope 1 fuel combustion only (international maritime bunkers). Electricity upstream (scope 2) excluded. Green ammonia combustion CO2 = 0. No process emissions for shipping.
rollout_limit_notes: National adoption bound reflecting international vessel fleet age (~25-30 yr), IMO decarbonisation targets, port infrastructure, and fuel supply chain development.
availability_conditions: Available from 2030; coefficients and max_share = 0 before 2030.
evidence_summary: MAN Energy Solutions ammonia two-stroke engine data; IMO GHG strategy 2023; IEA clean shipping 2023; Yara/JERA green ammonia carrier project.
derivation_method: Energy coefficients from AES 2025 Table F1 international maritime bunkers (29.5 PJ, 2023-24) / estimated service 200,000 million_tkm = 147.5 GJ/million_tkm. Fuel split: 60% HFO (88.5), 40% MDO (59.0). NGGI check: 11.4 tCO2e/million_tkm × 200,000 / 1e6 = 2.3 MtCO2e.
review_notes: Zero-emission marine fuels remain at demonstration scale as of 2025. Availability from 2030 is optimistic; strong IMO regulatory pressure needed. N2O slip from ammonia combustion excluded in Phase 1; add in Phase 2.
candidate_expansion_pathway: Add N2O emission factor; distinguish ammonia, green methanol, and H2 pathways.
times_or_vedalang_mapping_notes: Maps to zero-emission international shipping demand node.

review_notes: Zero-emission marine fuels remain at demonstration scale as of 2025. Availability from 2030 is optimistic; strong IMO regulatory pressure needed. N2O slip from ammonia combustion excluded in Phase 1; add in Phase 2.
candidate_expansion_pathway: Add N2O emission factor; distinguish ammonia, green methanol, and H2 pathways.
times_or_vedalang_mapping_notes: Maps to zero-emission international shipping demand node.

state_description: Green ammonia-fuelled vessels as representative of the IMO 2050 zero-emission fuel trajectory (which may also include green methanol and green hydrogen). Not available before 2030. Energy intensity higher than HFO due to lower energy density and conversion losses (400 → 320 GJ/million_tkm, 2030-2050). Zero scope 1 CO2e if green ammonia assumed.
cost_components_summary: Annualised non-fuel operating cost per million tonne-kilometres (real 2024 AUD), excluding explicit energy commodity purchases and carbon costs.
input_basis_notes: Green ammonia vessel: 0 GJ/million_tkm before 2030 (not available); 400 GJ/million_tkm (2030) declining to 320 GJ/million_tkm (2050). Scope 1 CO2e = 0 (green ammonia combustion).
emissions_boundary_notes: Direct scope 1 fuel combustion only (international maritime bunkers). Electricity upstream (scope 2) excluded. Green ammonia combustion CO2 = 0. No process emissions for shipping.
rollout_limit_notes: National adoption bound reflecting international vessel fleet age (~25-30 yr), IMO decarbonisation targets, port infrastructure, and fuel supply chain development.
availability_conditions: Available from 2030; coefficients and max_share = 0 before 2030.
